comments: Flashback 1967 – The first in a series of “Flashback” shows dedicated to a particular year.
All the tracks on the show are from 1967 – apart from the Cream session (1966) and the third track of the FNC (1968).

transcript.text: On 275 and 285, also in stereo on VHF, this is National Radio 1.
Well, hello there. Thomas the Vance here, TV on the radio, and welcome once again to The Friday Rock Show.
And tonight, welcome to Flashback 67. The whole show is built around the rock stars of that particular point in history.
Now, if you don't remember it, it might be fun for you to listen. If you do, you've got a mile of nostalgia.
we've got sessions by cream the who jimmy hendrix and pink floyd and also your favorite classic
cuts from that year 67 by the beatles velvet underground doors beefheart traffic stones
airplane country joe the mice eric burden the electric prunes the birds and others
but tonight to start off here's frank zapper to tell you what it was really like
Roger McGuinn, a.k.a. Jim McGuinn and Chris Hillman and David Crosby
in a track from the only LP that the Byrds had out way back in 1967.
The album was called Younger Than Yesterday
and the track you heard there was called So You Want To Be A Rock'n'Roll Star.
And we played it specially for Susan Harper
at No. 284 Thornborough Road in Colville
with lots and lots of love from Graham Winter of Keswick in Cumbria.
And before that you heard a track called Who Needs The Peace Corps
from the second album that came out by Frank Zappa and the Mothers of Invention
who incidentally used to be, before they got that sort of name applied to them,
used to be known as the Soul Giants.
The track, as I said, was called Who Needs the Police Call.
The album was called We're Only In It For the Money.
Did you know that Frank Zappa did ten days in jail for recording a sex tape?
Not particularly bad, I suppose, in its own way.
The only trouble is that he sold it to the police department.
The young lad from Walker-on-Tyne up there in and around Newcastle.
The track that came out on the album Winds of Change way back in 1967.
Eric Burden and The New Animals and Good Times.
And that really marked the change in his career from the boozy fella
to the gentle-minded, or rather mind-expanded flower-power type.
And I played that especially because Rory, the Irish dentist, sent me a nice card.
Wants me to say hello to Sharon.
And also wants to be remembered to all the people that convene in a youth hostel in Corfu.
People from Glasgow and Edinburgh, Newcastle, Bradford, Rochdale, Solihull, London, etc.
And I played that as well for Chris and Paula Cassidy
36 Wayman Road in Blackpool

It was issued as a single
It was actually the second single for the Electric Prunes
It was a track on their first LP
And it was called, as I said, Get Me To The World On Time
That was for John Wingfield of 32 Norfolk Drive, St. Leonard's, East Sussex
And before that, the thoughts of MLS Davjak
Keith Emerson, Blinky Davison, Lee Jackson and David O'List
From the album of the same name

way back in 1967 the pink floyd from their first album called the piper at the gates of dawn and
the track was called interstellar overdrive played that for everybody who wrote in and that was
many, many, many people.

but you know who you are.
I'll just read out one card
because it's really nicely put together.
It really is beautifully illustrated,
said he in his poshest English.
It comes from Mark Hutchinson
of No. 45 Enfield Drive,
Wibsby, I think it is, Bradford.
He played for all the Floyd fans around the country

It's an amazing cacophony, isn't it?
And they're still doing it.
Gigs in June.
We'll let you know when the tickets go on sale
and the best of luck in trying to get hold of some.
Something like five gigs, I think, here in London.
And also an open-air concert, possibly.
More from the Floyd later, from that particular era of 67,
as indeed you heard Days of Future Past from 1967.
Or rather, the track called Tuesday Afternoon from that album of the same name.

1967, White Rabbit.

done by Jefferson Airplane.

not the one that came out in Britain, only the American one,

off the LP Nils Loughlin, Shine On, You Crazy Diamond from Wish You Were Here by the Pink Floyd,
and How Do You Sleep by John Lennon from Imagine.

or Richards if you like, Keith Don't Go.
And the card that we pulled out of the bag of enormous right answers
was the one from Jack Haynes of 202 East Lancashire Road, Swinton, Manchester.
Mr. Haynes, you get yourself a £10 record total.
Congratulations.
Also, of course, I asked you to put together a Friday Night Connection that related to 1967.
Well, Jack has done something that relates to 1967 and some other period in history as well.

has a little bit of a catch in this,

Friday Night Connection for tonight.

formed in 66, they broke up in 1970.

thank you Paul, and the producer was Tony Wilson.
